summary:
Peisistratos (Pisistratus, c. 600–527 BC) was the Athenian tyrant whose three separate seizures and consolidations of power (561, 556, and 546 BC) shaped Athens at a crucial transitional moment between Solonian reforms and Cleisthenic democracy. His final tyranny (546–527 BC) was his most stable and productive: he maintained the Solonian constitutional framework while effectively controlling the state through loyal allies in key positions, confiscated land from aristocratic opponents, and pursued both economic development and cultural patronage.

Peisistratos systematically enhanced Athenian prestige and identity: he purified the island of Delos (removing graves from within sight of the Apollo sanctuary), reorganized the Panathenaic festival and the Dionysia (where tragic drama developed), commissioned public works including the first Athenian coins bearing the owl of Athena, and according to tradition organized the authoritative text of Homer. His popularity with the rural Attic peasantry — who benefited from loans, reduced taxes, and traveling circuit judges — gave him a social base independent of the aristocratic factions. His son Hippias's tyranny continued until the Persian-backed restoration attempt was foiled at Marathon in 490 BC, and Hippias guided the Persians there.
